title: An algebraic algorithm for non-commutative rank over any field
speaker: K.V. Subrahmanyam

proof_stages.label: Matrix-space null-cone and shrunk-subspace setup
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates the input as an m-tuple of n-by-n matrices and explains, through invariant theory, PIT, and Hall-type obstructions, that singularity is captured by subspaces U,U' with every A_i U contained in U' and dim U'>dim U failing, equivalently dim U' strictly smaller than dim U.

proof_stages.label: Noncommutative rank and its rank-theoretic avatars
proof_stages.summary: Noncommutative rank is defined as n minus the largest shrunk-subspace deficit, related to null-cone membership and rank over the free skew field, and contrasted with ordinary matrix-space rank.

proof_stages.label: Augmenting-rank strategy via second Wong sequences
proof_stages.summary: Starting from a matrix in the span, the algorithm tries to increase its rank; if the associated recursively defined preimage/image sequence stabilizes inside the image of the current matrix, its preimage gives a shrunk-subspace certificate that the current rank is already the noncommutative rank.

proof_stages.label: Rank augmentation in simple and pencil cases
proof_stages.summary: If stabilization fails, an augmenting sequence pulls a kernel vector out of the image; for length one and for matrix pencils, determinant arguments give a scalar specialization with larger rank, while general matrix families exhibit interfering commuting terms.

proof_stages.label: Noncommuting blow-up to isolate the augmenting product
proof_stages.summary: The interference is removed by tensoring with carefully placed matrix units in a D-by-D blow-up, so the desired product appears without competing terms and the matrix-pencil rank theorem yields a rank increase in the blown-up space.

proof_stages.label: Skew-field implementation, coordinate descent, and regularity
proof_stages.summary: To make the blow-up constructive, suitable skew fields are represented inside matrix algebras over extensions, the higher-rank matrix is rewritten in a division-algebra basis, coordinate reduction brings coefficients back to the base field, and regularity ensures ranks occur in multiples of the blow-up size.

proof_stages.label: Bounded blow-ups and polynomial-time algorithm
proof_stages.summary: The rank-increase procedure is iterated, and the Derksen–Makam blow-up reduction shows that blow-up sizes need not grow beyond about n, converting the original exponential augmentation scheme into a polynomial-time algorithm for noncommutative rank.

examples.name: Hall blocker in a bipartite graph
examples.note: A large zero block in a permuted bipartite adjacency matrix is used to make the shrunk-subspace obstruction concrete and to relate it to failure of perfect matching.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker analyzes a Hall blocker block in a bipartite adjacency matrix, deriving the perfect-matching obstruction and translating it into a subspace-shrinking/rank bound.

passes.reason: A Hall blocker is used as an obstruction witnessing failure of a perfect matching, but is not structurally sporadic, extremal, degenerate, or maximally symmetric.
A Hall blocker is a zero/empty bipartite subblock whose size violates Hall’s condition and obstructs a perfect matching.
the zero Hall block is an edge-missing degenerate obstruction certifying failure of a perfect matching
A Hall blocker is a large zero edge block witnessing failure of a perfect matching.
A Hall-violating zero block is used as an obstruction certifying nonexistence of a perfect matching, without being structurally sporadic or extremal.
A Hall blocker is a deficient zero-block/missing-edge configuration used as an obstruction to a perfect matching.
a Hall blocker is a large all-zero bipartite block used as an obstruction to the existence of a perfect matching.
a Hall blocker is a large zero rectangle, serving as an empty-edge obstruction certifying failure of a perfect matching.
a Hall blocker is an all-zero deficient rectangle used as an obstruction witnessing failure of Hall’s condition and hence no perfect matching
A Hall blocker is used as an obstruction certifying failure of a perfect matching, and structurally is a deficient zero-block configuration.
A Hall-deficient zero block is used as an obstruction certifying failure of a perfect matching, and the blocker itself is an empty block of adjacencies.
Used as a Hall obstruction to a perfect matching, and structurally it is a large all-zero edge-free block.

examples.name: Odd-dimensional skew-symmetric matrix space
examples.note: The skew-symmetric matrices show that ordinary rank can be smaller than noncommutative rank, and the later 3-by-3 computation exposes the cancellation that forces the move to noncommuting coefficients.
examples.engagement_reason: After citing odd skew-symmetric spaces as a counterexample, the speaker works through the 3-by-3 skew-symmetric case, normalizing matrices and computing products/powers to see why the rank-augmentation attempt fails while noncommutative rank is full.

passes.reason: The odd-dimensional skew-symmetric matrix space is used as the standard counterexample where ordinary rank is below noncommutative rank, and its elements have the maximal possible even rank just short of full rank.
Odd-dimensional skew-symmetric matrices are forced singular, and the space is used explicitly as the counterexample to equality of ordinary rank with noncommutative rank.
the highly regular odd skew-symmetric space consists of necessarily singular matrices with maximal even rank n−1 and is used to refute equality of ordinary and noncommutative rank
The full odd skew-symmetric space is highly regular and generically has the maximal singular rank n-1, used to refute equality with noncommutative rank.
The full alternating matrix space in odd dimension is highly regular with parity-forced upper rank n-1, and is used explicitly to refute equality of ordinary rank and noncommutative rank.
Odd-dimensional skew-symmetric matrix spaces are forced to contain only singular matrices and are used here as the explicit counterexample where ordinary rank is below noncommutative rank.
the odd-dimensional skew-symmetric matrix space refutes equality of ordinary and noncommutative rank, with every alternating matrix forced singular while the space is highly regular.
the odd skew-symmetric matrix space is a highly regular alternating-matrix family at the parity-forced rank boundary, used to refute equality of commutative and noncommutative rank.

the full odd-dimensional skew-symmetric space is highly regular but consists of necessarily singular alternating matrices, and is used to show commutative rank can be strictly below noncommutative rank
The odd skew-symmetric matrix space is presented as the standard case where ordinary rank is strictly below noncommutative rank, with every odd skew-symmetric matrix forced singular.

The odd skew-symmetric matrix space is explicitly used to refute equality of ordinary rank and noncommutative rank, and odd alternating matrices are forced singular with maximal possible rank only n−1.
Explicitly used to refute rk ≤ ncRk, and the odd alternating space is highly regular with maximal ordinary rank stuck at the boundary n−1.

examples.name: Quaternion coefficients for the 3-by-3 skew-symmetric example
examples.note: Replacing commuting scalar coefficients by quaternions makes the skew-symmetric example attain full noncommutative rank, illustrating why skew fields and matrix blow-ups can augment rank.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker applies quaternion coefficients to the 3-by-3 skew-symmetric example, computes the commutator term xy−yx=2z, and explains how this yields full rank via quaternion/2-by-2 matrix representation.

passes.reason: Quaternion coefficients are used positively to make the 3-by-3 skew-symmetric example attain full rank over a noncommutative division algebra, not to refute a claim.
Quaternion coefficients are used constructively as a standard noncommutative division-algebra device to make the 3-by-3 skew-symmetric example attain full rank.
quaternion coefficients are used constructively as a standard noncommutative division-algebra device to obtain full rank
The quaternion coefficients are an ordinary constructive noncommutative coefficient choice that makes the 3-by-3 example attain full rank.
Quaternion coefficients are a standard noncommutative division-algebra choice used positively to obtain full rank in the skew-symmetric example.
Quaternion coefficients are a standard noncommutative division-algebra device used constructively to make the 3-by-3 skew-symmetric example full rank, not to refute a claim.
quaternion coefficients are used constructively to make the 3-by-3 skew-symmetric example attain full noncommutative rank, not to disprove a claim.
the quaternion coefficients are used constructively to obtain a full-rank noncommutative lift of the 3-by-3 skew-symmetric example.
quaternion coefficients are used constructively to supply noncommuting scalars that make the 3-by-3 skew-symmetric example full rank, not to refute a claim
Quaternion coefficients are used constructively to obtain full rank for the 3-by-3 skew-symmetric example, not to refute a claim, and the quaternion algebra is classical rather than sporadic.

Quaternion coefficients are used constructively to obtain full rank in the 3-by-3 skew-symmetric example, while the quaternion algebra is a standard noncommutative division algebra rather than a sporadic or limiting object.
Quaternion coefficients are used as a positive noncommutative witness giving full rank, not as an obstruction or sporadic exception.
